title: Configure your app with environment variables

series.requires_description: Server must be locked down with firewalld, nginx proxying to Node app on port 3000

problem: Your app is hardcoding configuration values like port, database URL and API keys directly in the code. That is a serious problem — secrets end up in git, changing config requires a code change, and different environments need different values. Move everything out of the code and into environment variables.

setup.description: Check your running app and pm2 setup before starting.
setup.note: pm2 env 0 shows the current environment variables your app sees. Note what is there before you start.

hints: There are two ways to set env vars with pm2 — inline with pm2 start index.js --env production or via an ecosystem.config.js file. The config file is the proper way for real apps
Create an ecosystem.config.js at the root of your app — it defines your app name, script, and an env block with all your variables. pm2 start ecosystem.config.js to use it
Never commit secrets to git — put sensitive values in a .env file, add .env to .gitignore, and load them in ecosystem.config.js or directly in your app with the dotenv package
solution:
1. Check current env:
   pm2 env 0
   # See what environment variables your app currently receives.
2. Create ecosystem file:
   nano ~/node-hello/ecosystem.config.js
   # pm2 config file — the proper way to manage app config.
3. Add this content:
   module.exports = {
     apps: [{
       name: 'node-hello',
       script: 'index.js',
       env: {
         NODE_ENV: 'production',
         PORT: 3000,
         APP_SECRET: 'your-secret-here'
       }
     }]
   }
   # NODE_ENV, PORT and APP_SECRET will be injected into process.env.
4. Create .env file:
   nano ~/node-hello/.env
   # For sensitive values that must never be committed.
5. Add to .gitignore:
   echo '.env' >> ~/node-hello/.gitignore
   # Never commit secrets to git.
6. Restart with ecosystem:
   pm2 delete node-hello && pm2 start ecosystem.config.js
   # Reload pm2 with the new config.
7. Verify env vars:
   pm2 env 0
   # Confirm your variables are now visible to the app.
8. Check app still works:
   curl http://localhost:3000
   # App must still respond after the restart.
9. Save pm2 state:
   pm2 save
   # Locks in the new config to survive reboots.
10. Verify in app:
   printenv NODE_ENV
   # Confirm the environment variable is set.
